paragraphs.body: D. Discovery Writing a: write a letter to the author. Essay 2 Project Carlos Greaves: “Are You Allowed to Criticize Simone Biles?”

Hello Mr. Greaves, My name is Grant Huxley, and I’m enrolled in the University of Pittsburgh currently taking Workshop in Composition. I just read your essay recently, “Are You Allowed to Criticize Simone Biles?”, which I thought was formatted very interestingly and argues your case well for Simone Biles. What stood out the most to me while reading this essay was the writing style you choose to format this essay. I think it allows for a very humorous and engaging read, while still getting the point across very effectively. The repetition of “No,” at the start of each decision option effectively emphasizes your argument that attacks on Simone Biles are uncalled for and display bias from pundits. This essay reminded me of how frustrated I become when analyzing or reading arguments that are based off personal bias, and don’t contain a cohesive or solid argument. Often these arguments or talking points come from a place of ignorance. It reminded me to break down arguments or claims to the basic elements, to understand the aim of them, and if the way it is accomplished is valid. Some of the questions that occurred to me when reading this piece are, when did you first come up with the idea of a decision tree to format your essay? Was there another work or essay that influenced your decision? Another thought that made me wonder, was your intended audience the type of group that would laugh and enjoy the essay, or make them upset and angry? The sort of language that you employ in this essay makes this question difficult for me. Lastly, why were you inspired to write on this subject, was it a specific comment made on social media or the news, or just a general want to voice your support for Simone Biles? Thank you for creating this very engaging and interesting essay. It made me consider that there are so many alternative ways to argue a point than the conventional academic essay.

annotations.text: This essay reminded me of how frustrated I become when analyzing or reading arguments that are based off personal bias, and don’t contain a cohesive or solid argument. Often these arguments or talking points come from a place of ignorance. It reminded me to break down arguments or claims to the basic elements, to understand the aim of them, and if the way it is accomplished is valid.
annotations.comment: This is a really interesting connection you're making between the author's work and other reading/experiences you have done and had. Could you be more specific about one such argument/conversation?

annotations.text: Some of the questions that occurred to me when reading this piece are, when did you first come up with the idea of a decision tree to format your essay? Was there another work or essay that influenced your decision? Another thought that made me wonder, was your intended audience the type of group that would laugh and enjoy the essay, or make them upset and angry?
annotations.comment: These are really engaging questions that will give the author a strong sense of how interested you are in their writing.

You're doing a great job here of getting at the heart of the author's writing without sounding overly academic. Even though you mention early on that this letter is part of an assignment, your writing is engaging and reads as sincere. I really would like to know more about a specific argument that you have reconsidered as the result of reading this essay.
